title: MERIT Act of 2025
plain_summary: The MERIT Act of 2025 makes it much easier for government agencies to discipline or fire federal workers who perform poorly or break rules. The new law speeds up the process and removes some protections that workers used to have. New federal employees will have to work for two years instead of one year before they get full job protections. If workers do something wrong, agencies can take back bonuses they were given, and workers who commit serious crimes related to their jobs will get smaller retirement benefits.
last_action_text: Read twice and referred to the Committee on Homeland Security and Governmental Affairs.
subjects: Government employee pay, benefits, personnel management
Government ethics and transparency, public corruption

sections.title: Section 1  Short Title; Table of Contents
sections.text: This law can be called the Modern Employment Reform, Improvement, and Transformation Act of two thousand twenty-five or the MERIT Act of two thousand twenty-five. This section also lists all the other parts of this law and what they cover.

sections.title: Section 2  Termination of Authority for Chapter 43 Performance-Based Actions
sections.text: This section removes an old law about how government agencies could discipline workers for poor performance. The old system under section four thousand three hundred three is completely eliminated. However, if an agency already started disciplinary action under the old system before this new law takes effect, they can finish using the old rules. The section also updates other parts of the law to remove references to the eliminated system.

sections.title: Section 3  Adverse Actions Based on Performance or Conduct
sections.text: This section creates new rules for how government agencies can discipline or fire workers for poor performance or bad behavior. Agencies can take action if they can prove by a preponderance of the evidence (meaning it is more likely than not) that the worker's performance or misconduct justifies the punishment. When making decisions, agency officials must only consider specific factors like how serious the offense was, whether it was intentional, the worker's job level, past disciplinary record, work history, and any circumstances that might make the offense less serious. Workers get seven business days to respond to proposed discipline and agencies must make final decisions within fifteen business days unless they believe the worker committed a crime that could lead to jail time. Workers who get demoted start receiving the lower pay immediately and cannot be put on administrative leave during appeals unless they use their own saved vacation or sick time. Workers have the right to written notice of proposed discipline, the chance to respond in writing, legal representation, and a written decision with specific reasons. They can appeal to the Merit Systems Protection Board within ten business days. The section also speeds up procedures for disciplining supervisors who retaliate against whistleblowers from fourteen days to seven business days.

sections.title: Section 4  Prohibition on Grieving Adverse Actions and Reductions in Force
sections.text: This section changes the rules about what workplace disputes federal workers can file grievances about through their union contracts. Workers can no longer file grievances about serious disciplinary actions like suspension, demotion, or firing. They also cannot grieve decisions about layoffs or job eliminations. These types of disputes must now go through other legal processes instead of the union grievance system.

sections.title: Section 5  Actions Against Senior Executives for Performance or Conduct
sections.text: This section creates new rules for disciplining senior executives in government. Senior executives who get demoted from their high-level positions will only receive the pay for their new lower position, not their old higher pay. The section establishes that senior executives can be disciplined or removed if agencies can prove by a preponderance of the evidence that their performance or misconduct warrants such action. The decision-making process is similar to regular employees, considering factors like the seriousness of the offense and the executive's job level. Senior executives get seven business days to respond to proposed discipline and agencies must make decisions within fifteen business days. They have the same rights as other workers to written notice, the chance to respond, legal representation, and appeals within ten business days. The section also includes supervisors and career appointees who have completed their trial periods or were already covered by these protections.

sections.title: Section 6  Actions Against Supervisors for Performance or Conduct
sections.text: This section creates special rules for disciplining supervisors (workers who manage other workers). Supervisors can be disciplined using the same standard of proof and decision-making factors as other employees, but with special attention to their supervisory role and responsibilities. When supervisors are demoted, they must be given a position they are qualified for that the agency thinks is appropriate and that reduces their pay. Demoted supervisors immediately start receiving the lower pay and cannot get administrative leave during appeals unless they use their own saved time off. Supervisors have the same procedural rights as other workers including seven business days to respond and agencies having fifteen business days to make decisions. Agencies can discipline supervisors without first putting them on a performance improvement plan, and the regular performance evaluation procedures do not apply to these disciplinary actions.

sections.title: Section 7  Modification of Procedures for Furlough
sections.text: This section changes the rules about furloughs (forced unpaid time off for government workers). It creates two types of furloughs: general furloughs (for reasons other than lack of funding) and emergency furloughs (when Congress has not approved money to pay workers). For short furloughs of fourteen days or less, the section requires the Office of Personnel Management to create rules about when they can be used and what procedures workers get. For emergency furloughs when the government runs out of money, workers only get basic notice but no other procedural protections. For longer furloughs of more than fourteen days, workers get the same protections as other serious disciplinary actions, including the right to written notice, response time, representation, and appeals. The section also updates rules for administrative law judges to clarify that emergency furloughs do not require the same procedures as other disciplinary actions.

sections.title: Section 8  Reduction of Annuity of Employee Convicted of a Felony for Which an Adverse Action Is or Would Have Been Taken
sections.text: This section reduces retirement benefits for federal workers who are convicted of felonies related to their government job. If a worker is convicted of a serious crime that was committed while doing their official duties and the crime would have justified firing them, the time they worked while committing the crime does not count toward their retirement benefits. The head of the worker's agency makes this determination and must give the worker fifteen business days notice and ten business days to respond before issuing a final decision within five to fifteen business days. Workers can appeal this decision to the Merit Systems Protection Board within ten business days. The worker gets back any money they personally contributed to retirement for the time period that no longer counts. However, if the worker's spouse fully cooperated with law enforcement in the criminal case, the spouse can still receive survivor benefits. The section defines covered workers as those who were fired or quit while facing removal for the criminal conduct, and the felonious service period runs from when the bad conduct started until the worker was fired or quit.

sections.title: Section 9  Authority to Recoup Bonuses or Awards Paid to Employees
sections.text: This section allows government agencies to take back bonus payments from workers who engage in serious misconduct. If an agency head determines that a worker violated agency policy in a way that could result in firing or suspension of fourteen days or more, or violated a law that could result in more than one year in prison, the worker cannot receive bonuses for five years. If a worker already received a bonus and then commits misconduct during that same year, the agency can order the worker to pay back the bonus after giving them notice and a hearing. Workers must agree to pay back bonuses if ordered to do so as a condition of receiving them. The agency must allow workers to use a payment plan to pay back bonuses. Workers can appeal decisions about bonus eligibility or repayment orders to the Merit Systems Protection Board. Agency heads can create additional rules to carry out these provisions.

sections.title: Section 10  Extension of Probationary Period for Positions Within the Senior Executive Service
sections.text: This section extends the probationary period (trial period) for new senior executives from one year to two years. During this longer probationary period, new senior executives do not have the same job protections as permanent employees and can be removed more easily. This change applies to anyone who becomes a senior executive on or after this law takes effect.

sections.title: Section 11  Extension of Probationary Period for Employees in the Competitive Service
sections.text: This section extends the probationary period for most new federal employees from one year to two years. For jobs requiring formal training, the two-year period starts after training is completed. For jobs requiring licenses or certifications, it starts after the license is obtained or two years from hiring, whichever is later. For other jobs, it is simply two years from the hiring date. Agencies must clearly tell job applicants about probationary periods and their requirements. Supervisors must get regular reminders about when probationary periods end. Agency heads must certify in writing to the President when they decide to keep an employee after the probationary period. During probationary periods, employees have fewer job protections and can be fired more easily. The change applies to people hired after this law takes effect.

sections.title: Section 12  Application
sections.text: This section explains when this law takes effect and how it applies. Most parts of the law will start working one year after it is signed, unless a specific section says otherwise. The new procedures in this law will override any union contract rules that conflict with them. For purposes of this law, business day means any day except Saturday, Sunday, or federal holidays.
